summary:
The Algerian War of Independence, fought from 1954 to 1962, was one of the most brutal and consequential decolonization conflicts of the twentieth century. The National Liberation Front (FLN) launched coordinated attacks on French targets on 1 November 1954, beginning an insurgency against a French colonial presence that dated to 1830. Algeria was not classified as a colony but as an integral part of France, home to over a million European settlers (colons or pieds-noirs), which made the question of independence politically explosive in both Algeria and metropolitan France.

The French military response was massive and characterized by systematic torture, collective punishment, and forced relocations of rural populations. The Battle of Algiers in 1956 to 1957, in which French paratroopers dismantled FLN urban networks through methods including widespread torture, became internationally notorious and reshaped global debates about the ethics of counterinsurgency. Both sides committed atrocities: the FLN attacked civilian targets and killed Algerians suspected of collaboration with France.

The war destabilized the French Fourth Republic, ultimately bringing Charles de Gaulle to power in 1958. Despite initial promises to keep Algeria French, de Gaulle eventually negotiated Algerian independence through the Evian Accords of 1962. Following independence, approximately 900,000 European settlers fled Algeria, and thousands of Algerians who had fought for France (the harkis) were massacred or abandoned. Estimates of Algerian deaths range from 300,000 to over one million.

The Algerian War's legacy remains deeply contested in both France and Algeria, with debates over historical memory, torture, and colonial responsibility continuing into the twenty-first century. The conflict profoundly shaped Algerian national identity and contributed to the broader process of [[event:114|decolonization of Africa]].
